item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Image originally taken by Jennifer Vernon and disseminated under Creative Commons SA 3.0 with her permission.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here and is All Rights Reserved. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

The note recovered at the end of this article would indicate the original design of these odd sculptures was to help people see strange things that otherwise would be invisible. Whats an SCP about strange things that are invisible? SCP-2028! As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Hello, friends. theres a lot of strange things lurking in the desert, and we all know that. but soon, they might be moving in. hope this helps you spot them when they get here

To this:

Hello, friends. theres a lot of strange things lurking in the desert, and we all know that. but soon, they might be moving in. hope this helps you spot them when they get here

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The change looks alright to me.

The author of this page is still active, so you can PM them in regards to the rewritten sentence you mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I made a moderate sentence change that I was iffy about. Making a note of it here for posterity.
OK, so, the last sentence in the containment was so poorly-worded that I had to reread it several times to understand it. I when to Site19 in the IRC and posted the sentence by itself, and one user couldn't understand what was affecting what's perception until I explicitly explained it. Users agreed it would be acceptable to change it, so I did so. It is now correct (as far as grammar and general English rules go) and is easier to understand. I'd have rewritten it more to avoid the awkwardness altogether, but I feel that would have been far too much of a change to make without permission.

Apologies if this was out of line.
Edit: Site19, not site17. Derp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subjects will claim that these entities communicate with it, threatening to inflict severe bodily harm unless the subject settles in the town and has children. Subjects will claim to see children as small, emaciated humanoid figures obscured by dark smoke in the shape of the child's body.

Does it have a compulsive effect? Or do the subjects hallucinate the figures following them out of the town and harassing them until they settle there? Because without one of those two effects, I think 99.9% of people would stay as far away from the town as they could, and would never settle down there to have children.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, for the creep of how dreadful the lives of the children of Caja Negra must be, and the fact that they were forced to kill a man.

And Voct, I'm choosing to ignore what you say! I prefer it as inducer.

I also really didn't like the addendum, but the rest was strong enough for me not to care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subjects will claim that these entities communicate with it

Not sure what "it" refers to here.

By the end, I liked this. It plays off a particular fear of children, the parasitism aspect especially. I like that it only works in a single town, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Both of those scenarios are fine but it doesn't clarify.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I make edits to clarify

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…until their child is over the age of 11, at which point they will refuse to acknowledge the child's existence. Children will display normal behavior.

Normal behavior for average children, or for affection-starved emotionally abused children? Because that would make a big difference in how I saw this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a) ectopic pregnancy.

b) anomalously making her grow a new uterus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No it's not. It specifically says "pregnant" which is impossible without a uterus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So much ████████.

How are personnel supposed to follow the procedures if they don't know which town to avoid?

item.title: Re: Clarification
item.content:encoded: BAH! You ruined the confusion I was okay with having… Really tied the creep factor together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…this actually makes perfect sense. What is a child, when you separate yourself enough from the idea, but a parasite? That's all it is. It feeds off of you indirectly(unless you're a woman and carrying it, and thus, it is feeding off of you *directly*) and returns nothing (physical) to the host while it is still in its juvenile state.

However, this goes a step further. The people affected stop seeing their children as, erm, their children. They see them -just- as parasites. Things living off of them in bodies that are, honestly, something of a poor recreation of our(fully formed and adult) own. We love them because they're cute to us, as is any creature with the same proportions. The large head to body size, the eyes, a well-fed/healthy looking frame.

I honestly think this article might work better if the bit about the possibilities of aliens was taken out. Aliens are creepy, but not nearly as creepy as this just showing us the natural order of things.

item.title: Clarification
item.content:encoded:
For everyone's sake:

this is not an illusion-inducer. It's an illusion-breaker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to admit I've been waiting quite a while for someone to use this in an SCP. That being said, I'm afraid I can't go one way or the other with this. Just doesn't go enough in either direction, good or bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This needs cleanup. The description of the hallucinations isn't consistent, and there are some grammatical errors I'm not brave enough to try and fix from my phone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, but this feels more forced than anything, and it's not creepy enough to make up for that.

And that ending… is such a thing even possible?
